q: A medical-records pipeline extracts {patient_id, diagnosis_code, visit_date} from clinical notes. The prompt ends with 'Return only JSON, no other text.' but about 9% of responses still wrap the JSON in prose, include trailing commentary, or emit malformed objects, and these crash the downstream parser. The team wants the highest-reliability fix for production. What should they do?

options.A: Define the three typed fields as a tool / structured-output schema and validate every response at the tool layer, retrying on failure
options.B: Repeat the sentence 'Return only JSON, no other text.' three more times at the start, middle, and end of the prompt for emphasis
options.C: Set temperature to 0 so the decoding becomes deterministic and the model reliably emits clean, parser-safe JSON
options.D: Post-process each response with a brittle regex that grabs the substring spanning from the very first '{' to the very last '}' character and silently discards everything outside that range

explanation: Correct (A): the most reliable production pattern is forced structured output via a tool/JSON Schema, validated at the tool layer with retry on mismatch — the model is constrained to the schema rather than asked politely to comply, and the extra justification belongs here in the explanation, not the option. B just restates an instruction the model already sometimes ignores and adds tokens without enforcing structure. C is a common misconception: temperature=0 reduces randomness but does not enforce a grammar or fix instruction-following, so non-conforming completions still occur. D is brittle salvage — a regex cannot recover malformed or semantically wrong objects and silently mangles nested or escaped content.

q: A support-ticket router classifies tickets as 'billing', 'technical', or 'account'. Accuracy was fine on launch but has drifted: tickets mentioning 'charge' now land in 'technical' because they reference a charging cable. The single-line instruction is 'Classify the ticket into one of three categories.' What is the most effective first improvement?

options.A: Write explicit decomposed definitions per category with disambiguating cues, plus contrasting few-shot examples for the cable-vs-invoice 'charge' case
options.B: Raise temperature to 0.7 so the model weighs more candidate categories and considers a broader range before committing to one label
options.C: Train a dedicated fine-tuned classifier model and stand up a separate inference service for it before touching the existing prompt at all
options.D: Have the model emit a numeric self-reported confidence score for each ticket, then automatically route every ticket scoring below the chosen threshold into the 'technical' bucket by default

explanation: Correct (A): drift from ambiguous boundaries is fixed first by making the decision criteria explicit and decomposed, reinforced by contrasting few-shot examples that pin down edge cases like 'charge' — cheap and directly targeting the root cause. B raises randomness, which worsens consistency rather than fixing the classification logic. C jumps to heavy ML infrastructure before exhausting prompt-level fixes that are faster and often sufficient. D relies on LLM self-reported confidence, which is poorly calibrated, and arbitrarily biases ties toward one class.

q: An entity-extraction prompt for legal contracts produces {party_name, effective_date, governing_law}. When a contract omits a governing-law clause, the model invents plausible values like 'Delaware' or returns the literal string 'N/A', 'none', or 'unknown' inconsistently, breaking validation. What change most directly reduces this?

options.A: Specify each field's type plus an explicit absence rule ('if governing_law is not stated, set it to null') and make the field nullable
options.B: Lower the temperature all the way to 0 so the decoding is deterministic and the model stops hallucinating the missing governing-law field
options.C: Add 20 more few-shot examples of fully complete contracts so the model's overall extraction quality across every field improves
options.D: Instruct the model to never leave any field empty so that the returned object is always fully populated with some value

explanation: Correct (A): hallucinated or inconsistent absence formats are reduced by specifying field types and one sanctioned absence convention (null), enforced by a nullable schema, so the model has a single way to represent missing data. B does not address absence handling; a deterministic model will still fabricate or format inconsistently if never told what to do when a field is missing. C adds token overhead with examples of present fields and does not teach the missing-field behavior, which is the actual failure. D is exactly wrong: forbidding empties pressures the model to hallucinate values for genuinely absent fields.

q: A document-QA system places a 30-page policy manual in the context, then a long set of formatting and citation rules at the very top, then the user's question last. Reviewers find the model frequently ignores the citation rules buried above the manual. Which fix best addresses the likely cause?

options.A: Restate the critical citation rules right before the user's question and near any examples, where attention is strongest
options.B: Delete the system prompt entirely so that nothing at the top of the context competes with the policy manual for the model's attention
options.C: Set the decoding temperature to exactly 0 to force the model to deterministically obey the citation rules that currently sit at the very top of the long manual context
options.D: Remove all of the few-shot examples from the prompt so that the overall context is shorter and the rules are easier to reach

explanation: Correct (A): this is attention dilution / 'lost in the middle' — instructions far from the end of a long context get less weight, so the fix is to move or restate the critical rules adjacent to the examples and just before the user message. B throws away necessary task framing and does not change where the key rules sit relative to the content. C is the temperature misconception; lowering it does not relocate attention or fix instruction-following over long context. D removes useful guidance and still leaves the rules stranded far from the question.

q: A pipeline answers multi-step financial eligibility questions ('does this applicant qualify given income, debt ratio, and residency?'). It currently forces an immediate JSON verdict {eligible: true|false}. Accuracy on multi-condition cases is poor and the errors look like arithmetic/logic slips. What is the best change?

options.A: Let the model reason through the conditions step by step first — a reasoning field preceding eligible, or chain-of-thought ahead of the verdict
options.B: Raise the sampling temperature substantially so the model explores a far wider range of candidate verdicts and weighs more alternatives before settling on the final eligibility decision
options.C: Split the request into one API call per condition and concatenate the individual boolean results into the final eligibility verdict
options.D: Forbid any explanation and demand only the single boolean so the model stays focused purely on the eligibility outcome

explanation: Correct (A): complex multi-step decisions benefit from chain-of-thought — letting the model reason before emitting the structured verdict (reasoning field first, verdict last) reduces logic slips. B adds randomness, which does not help deterministic multi-condition logic and can worsen it. C fragments the task, loses cross-condition context such as interactions between debt ratio and income, and naive concatenation of per-condition calls won't reproduce the combined decision rule. D removes the very reasoning room that fixes the errors, optimizing for brevity over correctness.

q: A product-review analyzer must output {category, priority}. The team wired priority to be 'high' whenever the review's sentiment is strongly negative. They're puzzled that an angry one-star review reading 'I hate the new icon color' is flagged high priority while a calm note 'app silently loses my unsaved work' is flagged low. What is the core mistake?

options.A: Conflating sentiment with severity — priority needs its own decomposed criteria (data loss, security, blocked workflow), not emotional tone
options.B: The sampling temperature is set far too high, so the sentiment scoring comes out noisy and inconsistent across reviews and should be pinned to exactly 0 for stable, repeatable priorities
options.C: The prompt simply lacks enough few-shot examples of angry, strongly negative reviews to learn how to assign priority correctly to them
options.D: The model should emit a self-reported confidence score on its sentiment so that low-confidence reviews can be downranked in priority

explanation: Correct (A): sentiment is not the same as severity or complexity. Priority should be driven by explicit decomposed criteria — data loss, security, blocked core workflow — not emotional tone, which is exactly why the ranking inverts. B misattributes a definitional flaw to randomness; temperature=0 won't make tone equal severity. C adds examples of the wrong signal (anger), reinforcing the mistaken mapping. D leans on poorly calibrated self-reported confidence and still keeps sentiment as the basis for priority.

q: A nightly job extracts structured records from earnings-call transcripts. To 'maximize accuracy' an engineer set temperature=0 and is frustrated that the JSON still occasionally has the wrong field names ('rev' instead of 'revenue') and missing required keys. Which statement is correct?

options.A: temperature=0 only reduces sampling randomness, not schema compliance — use a validated structured-output schema with retry to enforce names and required keys
options.B: temperature=0 should already make the output perfectly schema-compliant, so the remaining field-name and missing-key errors indicate the API itself is malfunctioning
options.C: Raising the sampling temperature back up to around 0.5 or higher will make the model far more likely to settle on the correct field names and reliably stop dropping the required keys
options.D: Wrong field names and missing required keys cannot be addressed at the prompt or schema layer and require training a dedicated fine-tuned model

explanation: Correct (A): temperature=0 lowers randomness but is not a schema enforcer or a fix for instruction-following; structural compliance comes from forced structured output validated against a schema with retry. B is the misconception — determinism is not the same as correctness, and consistent errors are entirely possible at temperature=0. C makes things worse by adding randomness. D is overkill: schema-level enforcement (typed fields, required keys, validation plus retry) solves field-name and required-key problems without fine-tuning.

q: A prompt for generating release notes keeps growing: it now contains the role, 12 paragraphs of background, 40 few-shot examples, three overlapping tone guidelines, and a glossary — and output quality has gotten worse and more erratic, not better. What is the most reasonable corrective principle?

options.A: Avoid over-stuffing: trim to a clear role, task, and key constraints, keep only representative examples, and use delimiters so essentials aren't diluted
options.B: Keep adding even more few-shot examples to the already long prompt, continuing to append them until at least one included example closely matches every single release being written up
options.C: Set temperature to 0 to counteract the erratic, inconsistent output that the long and overloaded release-notes prompt has started producing in practice
options.D: Duplicate the three tone guidelines at both the start and the end of the prompt so that they become effectively impossible for the model to miss

explanation: Correct (A): over-stuffed prompts dilute the signal; the fix is to cut to a clear role, task, and constraints, keep a small set of representative examples, and structure with delimiters so key instructions stand out. B worsens token overhead and dilution — more examples don't fix a bloated, contradictory prompt. C is the temperature misconception; randomness isn't the cause here, prompt bloat and conflicting guidance are. D doesn't resolve the overlapping, contradictory tone rules; it just repeats noise.

q: A team adds eight few-shot examples to an address-normalization prompt, but the failures persist: the model still can't handle apartment-suffix formats it has never been shown, and per-request cost has climbed. Investigation shows the underlying instruction never defines how to format apartment suffixes at all. What is the best conclusion?

options.A: Examples don't substitute for a missing rule: define the apartment-suffix format explicitly, keep a couple of illustrative examples, and stop adding more
options.B: Eight few-shot examples is simply far too few for the model to generalize, so the right move is to scale up to roughly 50 hand-curated examples covering every known apartment-suffix format
options.C: Switch the decoding to temperature=0 so that the eight existing few-shot examples are applied deterministically and consistently to every new address
options.D: Remove the natural-language instruction text entirely and rely purely on the few-shot examples to teach the model how to normalize addresses

explanation: Correct (A): when the root cause is an undefined rule, more few-shot examples add token overhead without fixing it; you state the rule explicitly and use a couple of examples to illustrate. B keeps treating a missing-specification problem as a coverage problem, ballooning cost and still missing unseen formats. C confuses determinism with having a rule to apply. D discards explicit instruction in favor of inference-by-example, which is exactly the weak strategy that already failed.

q: An agent must emit a strict JSON action object every turn, but it sometimes opens with 'Sure, here's the action:' before the JSON, breaking the executor. The team can prefill the start of the assistant message. What is the most appropriate technique, and its main caveat?

options.A: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening '{' so it continues straight into JSON — but still validate, since shaping the start doesn't guarantee structure
options.B: Prefill the entire expected JSON action object in the assistant turn so that the model is left with essentially nothing further to generate on its own
options.C: Raise the sampling temperature for the agent on every turn so that the conversational 'Sure, here's the action' preamble becomes statistically less likely to appear before the JSON object
options.D: Add the instruction 'do not say Sure' to the system prompt as the single fix, and skip response validation since the preamble is now prohibited

explanation: Correct (A): prefilling / response shaping by seeding the assistant turn with '{' steers the model straight into JSON and suppresses preambles; you still validate because shaping the opening doesn't guarantee a complete, schema-valid object. B defeats the purpose — prefilling the whole object means the model produces no actual decision. C is the temperature misconception; randomness isn't why the preamble appears, and lowering it doesn't reliably remove it. D relies on a single brittle instruction and, by skipping validation, leaves the executor exposed to any non-conforming output.

q: A resume-parsing service returns {name, years_experience, skills[]}. Reviewers notice years_experience is sometimes a string ('5 years'), sometimes a number, and sometimes a range ('3-5'), and skills is occasionally a comma-joined string instead of an array. Downstream code expects an integer and a list. What is the strongest fix?

options.A: Encode the contract in a schema (years_experience integer, skills array of strings) with rules for ambiguous inputs, and validate plus retry on type mismatch
options.B: Add a polite note in prose asking the model to 'please use numbers and lists' for those fields, then parse the free-text output the same way as before
options.C: Set the decoding temperature to exactly 0 so that generation is fully deterministic and the model reliably converges on one consistent format for both years_experience and skills entirely on its own
options.D: Accept whatever shape the model returns and write defensive parsing branches for every years_experience and skills variant the team has observed so far

explanation: Correct (A): specifying field types in a schema (integer, array of strings) plus explicit handling for ambiguous inputs, enforced by validation and retry, is what guarantees the downstream-expected types. B is the unreliable 'ask in prose then parse' pattern that produced the inconsistency. C is the temperature misconception — determinism doesn't impose a type contract, and the model may consistently pick the wrong type. D is endless brittle whack-a-mole that breaks on the next unseen variant instead of enforcing the contract at the source.

q: A long compliance prompt is organized as one continuous wall of text mixing the role, the source documents, the rules, and the user's request, and the model frequently treats the source documents as instructions (or vice versa). Without changing the model or temperature, what is the most effective structural change?

options.A: Wrap each part in clear delimiters (XML-style tags like <role>, <documents>, <rules>, <question>) to separate instructions from data, with the rules near the question
options.B: Merge every section together into a single uninterrupted paragraph so that the model reads the whole prompt as one coherent, continuous instruction block
options.C: Set the decoding temperature to exactly 0 so that generation is fully deterministic and the model reliably stops confusing the embedded source documents with the actual compliance instructions it must follow
options.D: Delete the source documents from the prompt entirely so that there is no longer any document text left for the model to confuse with the instructions

explanation: Correct (A): XML/section delimiters give the model explicit structure to distinguish instructions from data, and putting the rules near the question counters attention dilution — both are prompt-structure fixes that don't touch the model or temperature. B removes the very boundaries that prevent confusion. C is the temperature misconception; instruction/data confusion is a structure problem, not a randomness problem. D destroys the inputs the task needs, 'solving' the confusion by removing the work.
